text: adequate mobilization of descending and transverse colon that is a splenic flexure is the most essential part for a proper anastomosis in a ultra low rectal cancer.
The surgeon lifts the sigmoid colon to identify the inferior mesenteric artery, then divides the peritoneum at the sacral promontory to reveal the left ureter.
The surgeon identifies the autonomic nerves and the inferior mesenteric artery, preparing to clip the artery while ensuring the nerves are visible and preserved.
The surgeon uses a hemlock clip to divide structures near their root, carefully safeguarding the autonomic nerves by pushing them downward.
The surgeon pushes down the gonadal vessels and elevates the descending colon towards the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein, cutting it at the highest possible point to ensure adequate mobilization.

The surgeon visualizes the gonadal vein, drops it down, and proceeds to divide the transverse mesocolon until the pancreatic body and tail are visible. The dissection continues along the root of the mesocolon adjacent to the pancreatic tail, ensuring no important structures are encountered.
The surgeon continues to divide and lift the mesocolon of the distal transverse colon, progressing towards the spleen, with visible separation of the mesocolon from surrounding structures.
The surgeon navigates above the upper pole of the kidney, carefully working through a loose area of tissue near the perinephric fat.
The spleen is visible as the surgeon lifts the splenic flexure of the colon, elevating it from its position.
The surgeon continues to divide the transverse mesocolon medially, opening the lesser sac and revealing the stomach.
The surgeon has completely mobilized the distal transverse colon off the retroperitoneum, with the colon visibly separated from the underlying structures.

In laparoscopy it is difficult that once you do anastomosis and when you find that there is a stretch on the anastomosis, a pull on the anastomosis, then again to go back and revert and do additional mobilization is quite difficult.
The surgeon returns to the pelvic region and identifies the left autonomic nerve, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the rectum while identifying the right autonomic nerve, remaining anterior and medial to it to ensure the ureters are safeguarded during the procedure.
The surgeon uses an energy device, either monopolar or harmonic, to dissect within the proper anatomical plane, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon continues dissection into the pre-circular hollow, targeting the loose areolar tissue in a female patient.
The surgeon navigates to the right side of the pelvic cavity, carefully remaining medial to the right autonomic nerve bundle to avoid nerve damage during dissection.
The surgeon progresses lower into the pelvic region, encountering visible blood vessels that require ligation using a vascular ligating instrument, such as a harmonic or ligature.
The surgeon has completed the removal of the mesocolic and mesorectal tissues, with the surgical field showing the absence of these structures.
So there is no violation of the mesorectal plane propagated by Hill in the early eighties.
The surgeon continues dissection until reaching the pelvic floor, where the muscles are visible and clean of surrounding tissue.

The surgeon completes the dissection on the right side, then proceeds to the left side, focusing on the remaining left lateral dissection. The posterior dissection has been mostly completed from the right side.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter and divides the peritoneum medial to the ovarian pedicle, pushing the ovarian pedicle laterally. The autonomic nerve bundles are safeguarded as the lateral support of the rectum is divided to encompass all lymphatic tissues. The dissection progresses lower and more laterally, below the level of the main nerve bundles along the lateral pelvic wall.

The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator to lift the uterus in a female patient, or alternatively sutures the uterus to the anterior abdominal wall with an ethylene suture. The tissue between the posterior vaginal wall and the anterior wall of the rectum is being divided.
The surgeon continues dissection in the proper plane, moving laterally to clear all mesorectal tissues. Fibrofatty tissues are removed, ensuring no lymphatic tissues remain.
The surgeon identifies the level of rectal dissection, thinning out the rectal tube by dividing the lateral fatty tissues to minimize the use of staples or cartilage.
cartridges or the staples that you fire, you are going to have staple over staple which will increase the risk of ischemia and which will increase the risk of leakage.
The surgeon narrows the rectal tube without compromising its vascularity, preparing to divide the tube at the same level where it has been narrowed.

The surgeon performs a low division of the rectum, confirming by palpation that the division is well beyond the small tumor. A single firing of the stapler, using either a green or gold cartridge, is employed to avoid overlapping staples.
In a narrow male android pelvis, the surgeon may require multiple firings of the stapler to effectively divide the low rectum.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to detach the left lateral peritoneal attachment of the descending colon.

The surgeon divides the distal half of the gastrocolic ligament, having already freed the mesocolon from the retroperitoneal area. The division is focused on the left half of the gastrocolic ligament.
The surgeon uses a small suprapubic incision to bring out the specimen, inserts it into the colon, and performs the anastomosis. The remaining colon is then easily maneuvered into the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon inspects the anastomotic line to confirm the completeness of the donuts. A leak test is performed to ensure there is no leakage at the anastomosis site, confirming the integrity of the surgical connection.
